The producer who worked on my album just passed away. How should I address this publicly? [Context: {"currentModule":"publicist"}]

This requires genuine, human handling — not PR strategy.

1. TIMING: Post when you're ready, not when PR logic says to. Authenticity matters more than speed here.

2. THE STATEMENT:
- Personal and genuine. Share a specific memory or what they meant to you.
- Acknowledge their talent and contribution. If they worked on your album, honor that publicly.
- Keep it handwritten/typed — no designed graphics or promotional formatting.
- DO NOT include streaming links, pre-saves, or any promotional material in the tribute.

3. FORMAT: A text post or a personal photo of you together. Not a career photo, not artwork.

4. ABSOLUTELY DO NOT:
- Use their passing to promote your music ('stream our collaboration')
- Make it about you more than about them
- Post before checking with their family/management about what they'd want shared

5. FOLLOW-UP: If their family sets up a memorial fund, amplify it. Offer to contribute to any tribute events.

This is a human moment, not a PR moment. The best thing you can do is be genuine and respectful.

I have a headlining show at a great venue. How do I get press to cover it? [Context: {"currentModule":"publicist"}]

Live show press coverage strategy:

1. PRE-SHOW PITCH (2-3 weeks ahead):
- Write a 'show preview' pitch to local press: 'What to expect at [artist]'s headlining show at [venue] on [date]'
- Include: story angle, notable support acts, any special production

2. PRESS GUEST LIST:
- Invite 5-10 local journalists/bloggers to the show (free +1)
- Include photographers — more likely to attend AND their photos become your press assets
- Send personalized invitations, not mass emails

3. PHOTO PASS:
- Coordinate with the venue for press photo passes
- First 3 songs is standard (photographers shoot first 3 songs, then leave the pit)

4. POST-SHOW:
- Follow up with attending press within 48 hours: 'Thanks for coming! Photos available at [link]. Happy to answer any follow-up questions for a review.'
- Share any coverage across your platforms

5. REVIEW STRATEGY:
- Not every outlet does live reviews, but local alt-weeklies often do
- A great live review is one of the most valuable PR assets — it proves you can perform, not just record

How do I write a pitch email to blogs to get my new single covered? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true,"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"emerging","genre":"indie-pop"}}]

Music journalists receive 200+ pitches daily. Your email has 5 seconds to stand out. Here's the formula:

**The Pitch Email Structure:**

**Subject Line (CRITICAL — determines open rate):**
- Format: `[Artist Name] — New Single "[Song Title]" | [One compelling hook]`
- Example: `Luna Park — New Single "Eclipse" | Indie-pop meets shoegaze, 500K streams on debut EP`
- Never: 'Please check out my music' or 'New music for review'

**Body (Keep to 150-200 words MAX):**

```
Hi [First Name],

[1-sentence personalized hook referencing their work]
Example: 'Your recent feature on Phoebe Bridgers' production techniques resonated with my own approach to atmospheric pop.'

[1-2 sentence artist intro with a unique angle]
Example: 'I'm Luna Park, an indie-pop artist from Portland producing entirely on a Tascam 4-track cassette recorder. My debut EP reached 500K streams without any label support.'

[2-3 sentences about the new single — STORY, not description]
Example: 'My new single "Eclipse" was written during a power outage and recorded by candlelight on that same Tascam. It's a meditation on what we lose when the lights go out — sonically influenced by Beach House and Alvvays.'

[Call to action]
'The single releases [DATE]. I'd love for [Publication Name] to premiere it. Private link and press assets below.'

[Links: private stream, press photos, EPK]
[Social links]
[Contact info]
```

**Targeting Rules:**
- Only pitch publications that cover your genre. Research their recent reviews.
- Pitch 2-3 weeks before release date (gives editorial time to write)
- Follow up ONCE after 5-7 days. No more.
- Personalize EVERY email. Bloggers can spot mass emails instantly.

I need help registering my songs with BMI for royalties. [Context: {"currentModule":"publicist"}]

PRO registration and songwriting royalties go to Publishing — routing via indii Conductor. I handle press and media, not publishing rights. Common confusion since 'publicist' and 'publishing' sound similar — but they're entirely different domains. Publishing handles BMI/ASCAP registration, mechanical royalties, and songwriter rights.

I want to get my single on playlists. How do I pitch to Spotify editorial and what about independent curators? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true}]

Playlist pitching strategy — two tracks:

**Spotify Editorial (Spotify for Artists):**
- Submit through Spotify for Artists at least 7 days before your release date (earlier is better)
- Fill out every field: mood, genre, activity, instruments, cultural context — this data feeds their algorithm
- Write a pitch that explains the story behind the song, not just the genre
- Editorial placement is not guaranteed — but submission makes you eligible for algorithmic playlist consideration (Radio, Discover Weekly) regardless

**Independent Playlist Curators:**
- Find curators in your genre via SubmitHub, Groover, or direct research
- SubmitHub: pay-per-submission system; curators respond within 48 hours with feedback. Cost is $0.50–$2 per curator.
- Groover: similar model, stronger European curator network
- Direct outreach: find curators on social (Twitter/Instagram), engage genuinely before pitching

**What makes a pitch work:**
- Describe the song in 2 sentences — feeling first, then context
- Mention any traction already (streams, features, similar artists on their playlist)
- Always pitch songs that fit the playlist's existing vibe — curators reject off-brand submissions immediately

Write a press release for my tour announcement. 20 cities starting in September. [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Ember","genre":"Folk","monthlyListeners":75000},"currentModule":"publicist"}]

Drafting via write_press_release.

Structure:
- Headline: 'EMBER Announces 20-City North American Tour — Fall 2026'
- Subhead: The tour follows [recent release/milestone]
- Lead paragraph: Who, what, when, where, why — in 2 sentences
- Quote: A genuine, human quote from the artist about the tour
- Tour dates: Formatted as a clean, sortable list
- Ticket info: On-sale date, pre-sale code, link
- Boilerplate: 2-sentence artist bio
- Media contact: press email, management info

I'll also offer to create a campaign to track press pickup and generate tour-specific EPK assets.

What's an EPK? Do I actually need one? I feel like no one reads press kits. [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"emerging","genre":"indie-rock"}}]

An EPK (Electronic Press Kit) is your professional introduction to anyone who isn't a fan — music journalists, venue bookers, playlist curators, festival programmers, sync supervisors, and brand partners. You absolutely need one.

When a blog editor gets 50 pitches a day, the artists with clean, professional EPKs get read. The ones who send 'hey check out my SoundCloud link' get deleted.

**What your EPK should contain:**
1. **Bio (150 words and 500 words versions):** Short version for quick scanning, long version for features. Written in third person. Lead with what makes you different, not where you're from.
2. **High-res press photo:** Minimum one professional shot, landscape and portrait versions. Not a blurry selfie.
3. **Music:** 3-5 best tracks, either embedded or as streaming links. Lead with your strongest song.
4. **Stats that matter:** Monthly listeners, notable playlist placements, press coverage, and live show history (especially if you've opened for known acts)
5. **Social links + contact info:** The booking email shouldn't be your personal Gmail — get an artist@yourdomain.com
6. **Embedded video:** A live performance clip or official music video

**Where to host it:** A simple one-page website works (Linktree Pro, Carrd, or a dedicated page on your artist website). Not a PDF — journalists hate downloading files.

**Advocate take:** Your EPK is your resume. At your stage, opportunities live or die on the professionalism of your presentation. Spend 2 hours making a clean EPK and it works for you 24/7. Want me to generate one using `generate_live_epk`?

A journalist completely twisted what I said in an interview. The article makes me look terrible. Can I do anything? [Context: {"currentModule":"publicist"}]

Misleading quotes in press — this is unfortunately common.

1. ASSESS THE SITUATION:
- Was the interview recorded? If you recorded it, you have proof of what was actually said.
- Was anything technically incorrect? Factual errors are easier to get corrected than interpretive framing.

2. PRIVATE APPROACH (FIRST):
- Contact the journalist directly (not publicly). 'I feel the article misrepresented my comments on [topic]. The full context was [your actual point]. Would you consider updating the article?'
- Contact the editor if the journalist is unresponsive.
- Most reputable outlets will add corrections or clarifications.

3. PUBLIC RESPONSE (ONLY IF NECESSARY):
- If private approaches fail and the article is genuinely damaging, issue a brief clarifying statement on your own platforms.
- 'In a recent interview, my comments on [topic] were presented without full context. Here's what I actually said and meant: [full quote and context].'

4. DO NOT:
- Attack the journalist personally on social media
- Threaten legal action publicly (do it privately through Legal if warranted)

5. LESSONS: Request to review quotes before publication (some outlets will agree). Record all interviews yourself.

6. Route legal review to Legal via indii Conductor if the misrepresentation rises to defamation level.

I want to pitch my single to college radio and independent stations. I've never done radio before. Can you help me build this campaign? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true}]

Building your college radio and indie station campaign:

**The radio pitch package (what you need before outreach):**
1. **One-sheet** — artist bio, single info, key selling points, streaming links, contact info. One page, PDF.
2. **Press release** — official single announcement with quotes and context
3. **Radio edit** — if your single has explicit content, you need a clean version
4. **Digital delivery** — send via AirPlay Direct, DMDS, or direct MP3 link (not Dropbox)

**Targeting:**
- College radio (CMJ/Spinitron charts): Focus on stations with active music directors in your genre. Research their current playlists before pitching.
- Independent stations: WFMU, KEXP, KCRW accept unsolicited submissions via their music submission portals
- Genre-specific shows: Look for shows dedicated to your genre — these hosts are enthusiasts, not gatekeepers

**Timing:** Submit 4–6 weeks before your release date for best consideration.

I have a meeting with a booking agent next week. Can you make me a one-sheet to bring? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true}]

Running generate_pdf for a booking agent one-sheet. A one-sheet is a single-page document (literally one page) that a booking agent can scan in 30 seconds. It needs: (1) Artist name and genre descriptor ('Detroit indie R&B artist'), (2) Headshot or press photo, (3) Short bio — 2–3 sentences max, (4) Top streaming stats (total monthly listeners, total streams on biggest track), (5) Notable achievements (playlist placements, press quotes, TV/film sync if any), (6) Sample set list or performance capabilities, (7) Social follower counts, (8) Contact info — your email and phone, or your manager's if you have one.

Send me the content and I'll lay it out cleanly. The visual should match your artist brand — do you have a brand color and preferred font?

Should I make a PDF press kit or a Live EPK? What's the difference? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Aether","genre":"Dream Pop"},"currentModule":"publicist"}]

Live EPK every time — here's why:

PDF EPK:
- Static snapshot frozen in time
- Goes stale the moment your streaming numbers change
- Large file size for email attachments
- No embedded media (journalists can't HEAR your music from a PDF)

Live EPK (via generate_live_epk):
- Public URL that auto-updates when your brand kit changes
- Embedded streaming player (journalists hear your music instantly)
- Real-time stats pulled from your profiles
- One link in your email signature covers everything
- Mobile-friendly (journalists read email on phones)

I'll generate a Live EPK right now. It'll be accessible at a clean URL and will automatically reflect any updates to your press shots, bio, or streaming stats.

PDF still has a role: physical events, conferences, and as an offline backup. I can generate that too via generate_pdf as a complement.
